summary: Updated the calcprogress tooling so C/header functions marked with the `asm` keyword are not counted as decompiled source progress. The PR adds a source scanner for asm-marked functions, passes those symbol names into map parsing, accumulates their sizes as a correction, and subtracts that correction from calculated source code size. It also includes small Python cleanup and IntelliJ project exclusions for generated/build directories.
what_changed: Added `tools/calcprogress/src/find_asm_functions.py`, which recursively scans `src` and `include` inputs for `.c` and `.h` files and returns names of functions declared/defined with the `asm` keyword.
Changed `main.py` to call `search('src', 'include')`, pass the resulting correction symbol set to `Map`, and pass `dol_map.correction` into `calc_exec_progress`.
Extended `cw_map.Map` with a `correction` byte count and private `__correction_symbols` set. While parsing symbols, if a symbol name is in the correction set, its size is added to `self.correction`.
Changed `progress.calc_exec_progress` to accept a `correction` argument and subtract it from `src_code`, preventing asm-backed functions in C/H sources from inflating decompiled code progress.
Cleaned up Python style in existing calcprogress code by replacing `== None` and `!= None` checks with `is None` and `is not None`, and by changing `class Map():` to `class Map:`.
Removed commented-out debug section dumping code from `asm_util.py`.
smart_moves: The fix corrects progress accounting at the map-symbol level rather than trying to special-case final percentages only; this ties the correction to actual symbol sizes.
Scanning both `src` and `include` handles asm functions that may be present in headers as well as C files.
Using a set of correction symbol names gives cheap membership checks while parsing map symbols.
The change preserves existing assembly-section counting and layers the asm-function correction onto `src_code`, minimizing disruption to the rest of calcprogress.
decomp_lessons: Progress tooling must distinguish true decompiled C from C files that still contain inline or placeholder `asm` functions; otherwise reported progress can be overstated.
When decomp projects use asm-marked functions in source files, map parsing can be reused to recover exact byte sizes for progress corrections.
Keep progress corrections symbol-based where possible, because source-level detection alone identifies names but not contribution size.
Tooling changes that affect project-wide metrics should make the accounting path explicit: detect symbols, parse map sizes, apply correction during percentage calculation.
naming_conventions: New helper module is named `find_asm_functions.py`, directly describing its scanning role.
The map correction input is named `correction_symbols`, and the accumulated byte count is exposed as `Map.correction`.
Private storage for correction symbol names uses `__correction_symbols` inside `Map`.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Detect asm-backed functions by regex scanning source/header text for the `asm` keyword near a function signature and body.
Restrict scanner inputs to `.c` and `.h` files to avoid unrelated files.
Use CodeWarrior map symbol sizes to compute the exact byte correction for matched asm function names.
Subtract the correction from `src_code` after calculating total code and assembly code sizes.
review_feedback: No issue comments or review comments were present in the provided PR slice.
One review is recorded in the metadata, but no review body or inline feedback was included in the excerpt.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that modify `find_asm_functions.py` or the asm function regex for false positives or false negatives.
Check whether `calc_slice_group_progress` was later updated to apply the same asm-function correction to slice-level progress.
Search for later changes to `Map.correction` or `correction_symbols` to see whether symbol-name collisions across object files required refinement.
Review later tooling PRs for more robust parsing of C asm functions beyond regex scanning.
